title: Dart Fundamentals
objectives: Understand JIT vs AOT and the single-threaded isolate model

sections.markdown: Dart is the language behind Flutter — a typed, null-safe, object-oriented language that compiles JIT for fast development (hot reload) and AOT to native code for fast release builds.

rows: Types are non-null by default; ? marks nullable
Independent memory + event loop (no shared state)
Dev hot reload / native release builds

sections.markdown: Dart is single-threaded per isolate with an event loop. There's no shared-memory concurrency — heavy work runs in a separate Isolate and communicates via messages, which is why Flutter UIs avoid most lock/race bugs.
Doing CPU-heavy work (parsing a huge JSON, image processing) on the main isolate freezes the UI. Use compute()/Isolate.run() to offload it.
Prefer final/const and immutable data. const constructors let Flutter skip rebuilding and reuse widget instances — a real performance win.

flashcards.back: JIT powers hot reload in development; AOT compiles to native code for fast, optimized release builds.
Future = one async value; Stream = a sequence of async values over time.
Single-threaded isolates with an event loop; heavy work runs in separate isolates communicating by messages (no shared memory).

quiz.explain: AOT gives fast startup and optimized native code in release.
Streams emit multiple values over time; Futures emit one.

quiz.q: To run heavy CPU work without freezing the UI…
quiz.explain: Offload to another isolate; a Future on the main isolate still blocks during CPU work.

challenges.text: Write a null-safe function using ?., ??, and late.
Consume a Stream with async* and listen/await for.
Parse a large JSON off the main isolate with compute().
Build a producer/consumer using Isolates and SendPort/ReceivePort.

interviewQuestions: What is sound null safety in Dart?
JIT vs AOT and how it enables hot reload?
When and how do you use compute()?
